sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The game is about moving green cells. Click a green cell → it becomes G marker with g diamonds around a target gray cell. Click a g diamond → transfers green to that target. Need to figure out the correct target positions for all 5 green cells.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 36 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking green(19,24) didn't produce any g diamonds. The board is unchanged. This is strange - earlier clicking green(19,24) did produce g diamonds. Let me check if it's because the state is different now (one green cell already moved).
Wait, looking at the current state, there are 4 green cells: (18,29), (18,35), (24,41), (36,41). The green cell at (19,24) is NOT there anymore! It was at (19,24) before but now it's gone.
Wait no, looking at the ascii output:
Row 18: `WwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwNNwWWwwww`
The green cells are at (18, 29) and (18, 35). There's no green at (19, 24).
But the segmentation says N at (18,29) and (18,35). Let me check the original green positions again:
Original: (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
After moving (19,18) → (18,29): should have (18,29), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
But the segmentation only shows 4 N cells: (18,29), (18,35), (24,41), (36,41)
Wait, (18,35) is different from (19,36). And (24,41) and (36,41) match the original right-panel positions.
So somehow (19,24) and (19,36) became (18,35)? That doesn't make sense. Let me look at the ascii more carefully.
Row 18: `WwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwNNwWWwwww`
After the B at col 9:
- Col 10: W
- Col 11-14: wwww (gray)
- Col 15-16: WW
- Col 17-20: wwww (gray)
- Col 21-22: WW
- Col 23-26: wwww (gray)
- Col 27-28: WW
- Col 29: w
- Col 30-31: NN (green!)
- Col 32: w
- Col 33-34: WW
- Col 35: w
- Col 36-37: NN (green!)
- Col 38: w
- Col 39-40: WW
- Col 41-42: www (gray)
So there are 2 green cells in the left panel, at cols 30-31 and 36-37. The original positions (cols 18-19 and 24-25) are now gray.
So it seems like when I clicked the g diamond at (18, 30), it not only moved the green from (19,18) to (18,29) but also moved the green from (19,24) to (18,35)!
Wait, that can't be right. Let me check: the original green at (19,24) is now at (18,35)? But I only clicked one g diamond.
Actually, maybe the green cells are linked. When I click green(19,18), it shows g diamonds for ALL green cells that need to move. The g diamonds at [(18, 30), (19, 29), (19, 32), (21, 30)] might be the target for one green cell, but clicking one of them moves ALL green cells to their targets.
Hmm, but that doesn't match what I saw earlier. When I clicked green(19,24) initially, I got g=4, G=1. When I clicked green(19,18) after that, I still got g=4, G=1. So clicking different green cells produced the same number of g diamonds.
Wait, maybe the g diamonds always appear in the same positions regardless of which green cell I click. Let me check the earlier data.
From transition 28: clicking (19, 18) → g=4, G=1. The g diamond centers were [(18, 30), (19, 29), (19, 32), (21, 30)].
From transition 17: clicking (19, 24) → g=4, G=1. But I didn't record the g diamond positions for that one.
Let me check if the g diamond positions are the same regardless of which green cell is clicked.
Actually, I think what happened is:
1. Click green(19,18) → g diamonds appear around the target position, G marker at (19,18)
2. Click g diamond at (18,30) → ALL green cells move to their target positions simultaneously
This would explain why both (19,18) and (19,24) disappeared and appeared at new positions.
So the game works like this:
- Click any green cell to show where ALL green cells need to go (g diamonds mark the targets)
- Click a g diamond to execute the move
- The green cells at (19,36), (25,42), (37,42) didn't move, so maybe they're already in the correct position?
Wait, but (19,36) became (18,35). Let me check the original positions vs current:
Original: (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
Current: (18,29), (18,35), (24,41), (36,41)
Hmm, (19,36) → (18,35) is a shift of (-1, -1). And (25,42) → (24,41) is also (-1, -1). And (37,42) → (36,41) is also (-1, -1).
So ALL green cells shifted by (-1, -1)! That's a uniform translation.
Wait, let me check: (19,18) + (-1, -1) = (18, 17), not (18, 29). So it's not a uniform translation.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the left panel green cells moved to different positions within the left panel, while the right panel green cells stayed in place.
Original left panel greens: (19,18), (19,24), (19,36)
Current left panel greens: (18,29), (18,35)
That's 3 → 2 green cells. So one green cell was "consumed" or merged.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try clicking the remaining green cells to see what happens.
Actually wait, I clicked green(19,24) but nothing happened because (19,24) is no longer green! The current green cells are at (18,29), (18,35), (24,41), (36,41).
Let me try clicking one of the current green cells to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Clicking the g diamond moved green cells to new positions. Current greens: (18,29), (18,35), (24,41), (36,41). Need to understand the target configuration.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current green cells
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_cells = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        center_r = (min_r + max_r) // 2
        center_c = (min_c + max_c) // 2
        green_cells.append((center_r, center_c))
green_cells.sort()
print(f"Current green cells: {green_cells}")

# Try clicking the first green cell to see what happens
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': green_cells[0][0], 'col': green_cells[0][1]}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nAfter clicking green{green_cells[0]}:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'g', 'G', 'p'):
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: top-left=({min_r},{min_c})")

# Get g diamond positions if any
g_positions = []
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'g':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        center_r = (min_r + max_r) // 2
        center_c = (min_c + max_c) // 2
        g_positions.append((center_r, center_c))
g_positions.sort()
print(f"g diamond centers: {g_positions}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  N: top-left=(18,35)
  g: top-left=(18,42)
  g: top-left=(19,41)
  g: top-left=(19,44)
  g: top-left=(21,42)
  N: top-left=(24,41)
  N: top-left=(36,41)
g diamond centers: [(18, 42), (19, 41), (19, 44), (21, 42)]

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current green cells
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_cells = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        center_r = (min_r + max_r) // 2
        center_c = (min_c + max_c) // 2
        green_cells.append((center_r, center_c))
green_cells.sort()
print(f"Current green cells: {green_cells}")

# Try clicking the first green cell to see what happens
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': green_cells[0][0], 'col': green_cells[0][1]}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nAfter clicking green{green_cells[0]}:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'g', 'G', 'p'):
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: top-left=({min_r},{min_c})")

# Get g diamond positions if any
g_positions = []
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'g':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        center_r = (min_r + max_r) // 2
        center_c = (min_c + max_c) // 2
        g_positions.append((center_r, center_c))
g_positions.sort()
print(f"g diamond centers: {g_positions}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
